name: Rama
subtitle: Maryada Purushottama - The Perfect Man
description: Rama (Sanskrit: lord of virtue) is the seventh avatar of Vishnu and the central hero of the Ramayana, one of Hinduism's two great epics (composed by Valmiki, c. 5th-4th century BCE). As prince and later king of Ayodhya, Rama embodies dharma (righteous duty) in its most perfect form: he is the ideal son who accepts exile to preserve his father's honor, the ideal husband who rescues his wife Sita from the demon king Ravana, the ideal brother who inspires unwavering loyalty from Lakshmana and Bharata, and the ideal king whose rule became synonymous with utopia (Rama-rajya). His fourteen-year exile, alliance with Hanuman and the monkey army, and war against Lanka to rescue Sita constitute the Ramayana's main narrative. Rama is worshipped throughout India and Southeast Asia as the supreme exemplar of virtuous conduct.

symbols: The bow (especially the bow of Shiva, which he broke)

parentage.divine: Vishnu - As seventh avatar, Rama is Vishnu incarnate for the destruction of Ravana
parentage.mortal: Dasharatha - King of Ayodhya; Kaushalya - Queen-mother
parentage.heritage: Ikshvaku/Solar Dynasty; born from divine nectar distributed by Vishnu

family.wife: Sita - Daughter of King Janaka, incarnation of goddess Lakshmi
family.brothers: Lakshmana (accompanied him in exile), Bharata (ruled in his name), Shatrughna
family.sons: Lava and Kusha (twins, born during Sita's second exile)

weapons: Brahmastra - Divine weapon received from Vishwamitra
The bow of Shiva - broken at Sita's swayamvara

keyMyths.title: Birth and Breaking of Shiva's Bow
keyMyths.description: Rama was born to King Dasharatha after a fire sacrifice (putrakameshti yajna). Trained by sage Vishwamitra, the young Rama defended the sage's sacrifice and was taken to Mithila, where King Janaka offered his daughter Sita to whoever could string the bow of Shiva. Rama not only strung it but broke it, winning Sita's hand.

keyMyths.title: The Exile
keyMyths.description: On the eve of Rama's coronation, Queen Kaikeyi invoked boons from Dasharatha: crown Bharata king and exile Rama for fourteen years. To honor his father's word, Rama accepted exile without protest. Sita and Lakshmana insisted on accompanying him. This act of filial piety and dharma defines Rama's character.

keyMyths.title: The Abduction of Sita
keyMyths.description: In the forest, the demon Ravana, disguised as an ascetic, abducted Sita while Rama pursued a golden deer (actually demon Maricha). The dying vulture Jatayu informed Rama. This abduction sets in motion the epic's central conflict.

keyMyths.title: Alliance with Hanuman and the Monkey Army
keyMyths.description: Searching for Sita, Rama befriended the monkey king Sugriva and his minister Hanuman. Rama helped Sugriva defeat his brother Vali; in return, the monkey army joined his cause. Hanuman leaped across the ocean to Lanka, found Sita, and returned with news.
keyMyths.source: Valmiki Ramayana, Kishkindha Kanda & Sundara Kanda

keyMyths.title: The War Against Lanka
keyMyths.description: Rama's army built a bridge across the ocean to Lanka. In the great war, Rama slew Ravana's champions—Kumbhakarna, Indrajit—and finally defeated the ten-headed demon king himself with the Brahmastra. Sita was rescued after fourteen years.

keyMyths.title: Sita's Trial by Fire (Agni Pariksha)
keyMyths.description: To prove her purity after captivity in Lanka, Sita entered fire. Agni, god of fire, returned her unharmed, demonstrating her chastity. This controversial episode is interpreted variously: as Ravana holding only a shadow Sita, as patriarchal expectation, or as metaphysical truth.

keyMyths.title: Return and Rama-rajya
keyMyths.description: Rama returned to Ayodhya and was crowned king. His reign became known as Rama-rajya, the ideal kingdom where dharma prevailed, no one suffered, and justice ruled. This utopia remains the Hindu political ideal.

heroJourney.thresholdCrossing: Leaving Ayodhya for the forest, renouncing kingdom
heroJourney.ordeal: Sita's abduction; search across India; war against Lanka
heroJourney.allies: Hanuman, Sugriva, Vibhishana, the monkey army
heroJourney.transformation: From prince to exile to righteous warrior to ideal king

comparativeNotes.greek: Like Odysseus, Rama is exiled and must journey to reclaim what he lost; the faithful wife motif parallels Penelope and Sita
comparativeNotes.nearEastern: The demon-king antagonist parallels ancient Near Eastern chaos monsters; the divine hero defeating evil is universal
comparativeNotes.buddhist: The Ramayana is known throughout Buddhist Southeast Asia; the Dasharatha Jataka presents Rama as a bodhisattva
comparativeNotes.christian: Comparative theologians have noted parallels between Rama and Christ as divine figures who suffer and triumph

christianComparativeNotes.overview: Rama has been the subject of interfaith dialogue since the 19th century. While there is no patristic engagement (the Church Fathers did not know the Ramayana), modern comparative theology has explored meaningful parallels while respecting differences.
christianComparativeNotes.incarnation: Both Rama and Christ are divine beings who take human form—Rama as Vishnu's avatar, Christ as the eternal Word made flesh (John 1:14). Both descend to defeat evil and restore righteousness.
christianComparativeNotes.exileAndHumility: Rama's willing acceptance of exile to honor his father parallels Christ's kenosis—emptying himself of divine prerogatives (Philippians 2:6-8). Both choose suffering over power.
christianComparativeNotes.rescueOfTheBeloved: Rama rescues Sita (often interpreted as the soul held captive by demonic forces). Christian theologians have compared this to Christ rescuing the Church, his bride, from Satan's captivity.
christianComparativeNotes.victoryOverEvil: Rama's defeat of Ravana parallels Christ's defeat of Satan. Both represent cosmic victory of good over evil, restoration of divine order.
christianComparativeNotes.righteousKingship: Rama-rajya, the ideal kingdom, parallels the Kingdom of God. Both represent eschatological hope for perfect rule.
christianComparativeNotes.sufferingRighteousness: Both Rama and Christ suffer despite their righteousness—indeed, because of it. Their suffering has redemptive significance for others.
christianComparativeNotes.missionaryEncounter: 19th-century missionaries in India grappled with Rama's popularity. Some dismissed Hindu devotion; others (like Roberto de Nobili earlier) saw praeparatio evangelica in Hindu longing for divine incarnation and rescue from evil.
keyDistinctions: Rama is an avatar (descent); Christ is the eternal Son who takes on human nature permanently
Rama's mission is to defeat one demon king; Christ's mission is to defeat sin and death universally
Rama's suffering is imposed by circumstance; Christ's is voluntary atonement for sin
Rama establishes an earthly utopia; Christ's kingdom is 'not of this world' (John 18:36)
There is no concept of vicarious atonement in Rama's story; Christ dies for others' sins

abilities: Perfect dharmic virtue - Upholder of righteousness in every role
Supreme archery - Greatest archer of his age
Divine weapons - Mastery of celestial astras
Patience and endurance - Fourteen years of exile without complaint
feats: Broke the bow of Shiva at Sita's swayamvara
Accepted exile to preserve his father's honor
Slew the demon king Ravana with the Brahmastra
Built the bridge across the ocean to Lanka

sources.title: Valmiki Ramayana (c. 5th-4th century BCE): The original Sanskrit epic, 24,000 verses in seven kandas.
Tulsidas, Ramcharitmanas (16th century CE): Hindi retelling that made Rama devotion accessible to the masses.
Kamban, Ramavataram (12th century CE): Tamil version with distinct theological emphasis.
Adhyatma Ramayana (14th-15th century CE): Vedantic interpretation of the epic.
Various Southeast Asian versions: Thai Ramakien, Khmer Reamker, Indonesian Kakawin Ramayana.

modernScholarship: Robert Goldman (ed.), The Ramayana of Valmiki (1984-2017): Critical scholarly translation
Philip Lutgendorf, Hanuman's Tale (2007): Study of Hanuman devotion
Sheldon Pollock, The Ramayana of Valmiki, Vol. 2-3 (1986-1991): Scholarly commentary
achievements: Mastery of martial arts and weaponry
Winning Sita's hand in marriage by stringing Shiva's bow
Upholding dharma by accepting exile to the forest for 14 years
Building a bridge (Rama Setu) across the ocean to Lanka
Slaying Ravana, the ten-headed demon king, and liberating Sita
Establishing a just and righteous rule (Rama Rajya) upon his return to Ayodhya

associations.name: Monkey
associations.significance: Represents devotion, strength, and selfless service, exemplified by Hanuman.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Associated with divinity, serenity, and Vishnu, of whom Rama is an avatar.

associations.name: Bow and Arrow
associations.significance: Symbolizes power, precision, and Rama's ability to overcome obstacles.

associations.name: Tulsi (Holy Basil)
associations.significance: Sacred plant offered in worship to Rama, representing purity and devotion.

associations.name: Dharma
associations.significance: Represents righteousness, duty, and moral order, which Rama embodies perfectly.

associations.name: Ayodhya
associations.significance: Represents an ideal and prosperous kingdom ruled with justice and compassion.

companions.name: Sita
companions.description: Rama's devoted wife, an embodiment of purity, virtue, and wifely devotion (Sati). She is kidnapped by Ravana and rescued by Rama.

companions.name: Lakshmana
companions.description: Rama's loyal and devoted younger brother, who accompanies him during his exile and serves as his protector.

companions.name: Hanuman
companions.description: A powerful monkey god and Rama's most ardent devotee, known for his strength, courage, and unwavering loyalty. He plays a crucial role in finding Sita and assisting Rama in the war against Ravana.

companions.name: Sugriva
companions.description: The exiled monkey king who helps Rama find Sita and defeat Ravana. Rama restores Sugriva to his throne.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both undertake long journeys filled with trials and tribulations, demonstrating courage and resilience in the face of adversity.
Both are exemplary warriors known for their exceptional strength, skill in combat, and adherence to a strict code of honor.
Both embody ideals of righteousness and are figures of great beauty and virtue, though Baldur's story ends tragically.

worshipPractices.name: Rama Navami Celebrations
worshipPractices.description: Rama Navami is a major Hindu festival celebrating the birth of Rama. Devotees observe fasts, visit temples, and participate in processions, chanting prayers and singing devotional songs in praise of Rama. The Ramayana is often recited or performed.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Worship
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Rama are found throughout India and other parts of the world. Devotees offer prayers, flowers, and food to the deity. The temples serve as centers for religious and cultural activities, fostering a sense of community and devotion.

cultural.modernLegacy: Rama remains a potent symbol of righteousness, ideal kingship, and devotion in modern Hindu culture. His story continues to inspire art, literature, and political discourse, serving as a benchmark for ethical conduct and responsible leadership. The concept of 'Rama Rajya' (the reign of Rama) is often invoked as an aspiration for a just and harmonious society.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Rama was born in Ayodhya to King Dasharatha and Queen Kausalya, as the seventh avatar of Vishnu, destined to vanquish evil. From a young age, he exhibited exceptional qualities of righteousness, compassion, and strength. His education under the sage Vashistha honed his skills in warfare, statecraft, and philosophy. Rama's early life was marked by his obedience to his parents and his unwavering commitment to dharma. An important episode of his youth involves protecting the sage Vishwamitra's yajna (sacrificial ritual) from demons like Taraka and Subahu, showcasing his divine powers and courage. This period solidified his reputation as a protector of the innocent and a champion of good. Rama's virtuous character and exemplary conduct made him a beloved prince, admired by the people of Ayodhya.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Rama's most significant quest begins with his exile to the forest, a consequence of his father's promise to his stepmother, Kaikeyi. Accompanied by his wife Sita and brother Lakshmana, Rama spends fourteen years in exile, facing numerous challenges and encounters. The pivotal event is the abduction of Sita by Ravana, the demon king of Lanka. This leads to Rama's alliance with Hanuman and the monkey army, culminating in the epic battle against Ravana. Rama's determination to rescue Sita and uphold dharma drives him to build a bridge across the ocean and confront Ravana in a fierce war. His victory over Ravana symbolizes the triumph of good over evil. Rama's journey is a testament to his unwavering commitment to duty, love, and justice.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Rama's legacy transcends mythology, profoundly influencing Indian culture, philosophy, and art. He is revered as the ideal man (Maryada Purushottama), embodying perfect virtue, righteousness, and compassion. The concept of 'Rama Rajya,' an ideal kingdom characterized by justice, peace, and prosperity, continues to inspire political and social thought. The Ramayana, the epic narrative of Rama's life, has been retold and reinterpreted across centuries, shaping moral values and cultural identity. Rama's story serves as a timeless reminder of the importance of dharma, devotion, and the ultimate triumph of good over evil. His image and narrative continue to be celebrated through festivals, performances, and artistic expressions, ensuring his enduring presence in the collective consciousness.

shortDescription: Rama, the seventh avatar of Vishnu, is the heroic prince of Ayodhya. He embodies dharma and is central to the Ramayana epic, revered for his righteousness and courage.
